content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on the array any number of times:


Choose any subsequence SSS of the array AAA and a **positive** integer XXX such that XXX is a power of 222 and subtract XXX from all the elements of the subsequence SSS.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to make all the elements of the array equal to 000.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
First line of each test case contains an integer NNN denoting the length of the array AAA.
Second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ - denoting the elements of array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, the **minimum** number of moves required to make all the elements of the array AAA equal to 000.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to make all array elements equal to 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case compute the OR of all numbers and count its set bits. 2|2|2 = 2 (binary 10) → 1 set bit. 2|2|2|4 = 6 (110) → 2 set bits. 0|0 = 0 → 0. 1|2|3 = 3 (11) → 2.
OR = 31 (binary 11111) has 5 set bits, so 5 operations are needed.
All elements are already zero; no operation is required.

solution.approach: Observe that each operation subtracts a power of two (2^k) from a chosen subsequence. Subtractions on different bit positions are independent – a bit at position k can be cleared only by using the operation with X = 2^k. Therefore, for each bit position that appears as 1 in at least one array element, we need at least one operation. Conversely, we can achieve the goal in exactly that many operations by processing each set bit separately: for each bit k that is present in the bitwise OR of all elements, select all indices whose k‑th bit is 1 and subtract 2^k. After processing all such bits, every element becomes zero. Hence the answer equals the number of set bits in the bitwise OR of the whole array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case to compute the OR, plus O(1) for counting set bits. Overall O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides input storage.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data=sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it=iter(data)
    T=int(next(it))
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=int(next(it))
        or_all=0
        for _ in range(N):
            or_all|=int(next(it))
        out.append(str(bin(or_all).count('1')))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__=='__main__':
    solve()
